meta.title: Search box typing lag under product-card load
meta.summary: Typing gets laggy after the third character because every keypress does too much work and also triggers a request.
signals: Typing gets much slower after 3+ characters
Most time is spent filtering and highlighting before the screen updates
Every keypress also sends a network request

context.symptom: Search feels fine at first, but once there are more matching items, each keypress feels about 200ms late.
context.userImpact: Users think the input is missing characters and give up before finishing the search.
context.environment: React 18 catalog page with about 2,400 product cards. The app also asks the server for suggestions on every change.

evidence.title: Real-user timing
evidence.body: For slower real-world sessions, input delay jumps from about 40ms to a little over 200ms after the third character.

evidence.title: Profiler result
evidence.body: Most of the time is spent filtering items and building highlights before the screen updates.

evidence.title: Current handler

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What is most likely causing the lag?
stages.helperText: Pick the cause that explains both the heavy JavaScript work and the sticky typing.

options.label: Filtering and highlighting run directly on every keypress, so the screen cannot update quickly enough.
options.feedback: Correct. Too much synchronous work happens before the input can feel responsive.

options.label: Network latency alone is causing the input to freeze.
options.feedback: Network can slow the results, but it does not explain the heavy JavaScript work shown in the profiler.

options.label: The main problem is hover animations on the cards; the JavaScript path is mostly fine.
options.feedback: CSS can contribute, but the profiler points to JavaScript work before the screen updates, not hover styling.

options.label: Global keyboard listeners are leaking and duplicating each keypress.
options.feedback: There is no signal of duplicated handlers. The evidence points to heavy work, not repeated events.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: What would you check first? Put the best three at the top.
stages.helperText: First prove whether typing is blocked by local work, network delay, or both.

candidates.label: Profile a real typing session in React DevTools or the browser Performance panel.
candidates.description: See where the time goes before changing the architecture.

candidates.label: Mock the network and measure only the local filter and highlight work.
candidates.description: Separate frontend work from request delay.

candidates.label: Throttle CPU and network separately to see which one hurts more.
candidates.description: Find out whether the main problem is local work or request delay.

candidates.label: Rewrite the suggestions endpoint before measuring the UI.
Add a debounce immediately and skip profiling.
candidates.description: Common fix, but weak if you have not measured first.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which fixes should be in the first real patch?
stages.helperText: Pick the changes that remove the bottleneck instead of just masking it.

options.label: Increase the fetch timeout so requests stop failing under load.
options.feedback: This does not address input lag and can hide the real performance problem.

options.label: Debounce suggestion requests so network work is not fired on every single keystroke.
options.feedback: Correct. This cuts avoidable request churn and reduces work on the input path.

options.label: Disable StrictMode locally so typing feels smoother in development.
options.feedback: That can change dev noise, but it does not solve the production bottleneck.

options.label: Move expensive filtering and highlighting off the immediate typing path with deferred work or similar scheduling.
options.feedback: Correct. The main win is removing blocking work from the typing path.

options.label: Virtualize the result list so a large card grid does not rerender in full while typing.
options.feedback: Correct. Rendering fewer cards lowers total update cost once the query changes.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best way to catch this kind of regression again?
stages.helperText: Pick the signal that would warn you about sticky typing early.

options.label: Monitor only search API timing because slower requests caused the lag.
options.feedback: API timing matters, but it misses the blocking render work shown in the incident.

options.label: Have QA type quickly on release day and rely on that spot check.
options.feedback: Manual checks help, but they are too weak as the main guardrail.

options.label: Track search interaction latency and add a budget for typing responsiveness in the performance workflow.
options.feedback: Correct. This watches the actual user-facing failure mode instead of only backend timing.

options.label: Delete result highlighting permanently so the page can never regress again.
options.feedback: That throws away product behavior instead of building a durable performance guardrail.

scoreBands.label: You started fixing before isolating the bottleneck
scoreBands.summary: The lag was real, but you still needed to separate local work from request delay.

scoreBands.label: Good instincts, but the priorities were not fully clear
scoreBands.summary: You found useful fixes, but the debug order or guardrail missed the main user-facing signal.

scoreBands.label: You isolated the lag clearly
scoreBands.summary: You found the blocking work, chose layered fixes, and added a measurable guardrail.

idealRunbook: Profile the typing interaction before changing architecture.
Separate local render cost from request delay.
Reduce typing-path work first, then cut list rendering and request churn.
Track the delay users actually feel after rollout.

teachingBlocks.title: What this incident tests
teachingBlocks.text: Performance debugging gets easier when you first ask: is the delay coming from local work, network delay, or both?

teachingBlocks.title: Strong answer signals
items: You separate slow JavaScript work from slow network.
You pick a metric that matches what the user actually feels.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If this only happened on low-end phones, what would you measure first?
